content.full_text:
Re:
Fern PorrrlcAI- PRACTTcES ConarrarssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-56@ o Fax (916) 322-0886
October 22,2W
Randall W. Keen
Manatt Phelps & Phillips, LLP
l2l5 K Street, Suite 1900
Sacramento, CA 95814
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-0+219
Dear Mr. Keen:
This letter is in response to your request on betralf of the California Protocol
Foundation (*CPF') for advice regmding the gift provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act').1
QUESTTONS
l. May CPF pay the actual transportation and related lodging and subsistence
costs associated with a proposed trade mission on behalf of officials in the
Schwarzenegger administration, consistent with the Act's gift limits?
2. Who would be the source of the gift if CPF solicited and accepted donations
from private entities to defray the costs of the trip, and if CPF directs and conhols the use
of the donations, and the individuals do not earmark or direct theirpayments to any
clearly identifi ed offi cials?
3. May CPF extend an offer to the Governor's administration to pay for the trade
rnission, but consistent with regulation 18944.2, allow the Governor's administration in
its sole discretion to determine which specific officials mayparticipate in the trade
rnission?
I Government Code sections S1000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
rPg9


File No. A-04-219
Page No. 2
4. Would payments to the economic development and trade promotion account
established by the Secretary of Business, Transportation and Housing "for tlre purposes
ofpromoting international trade and invesftnent," be subject to the gift limits of the Act?
CONCLUSIONS
l. Thepayments would be exempt from the gift limits of theAct.
2. CPF will be considered the source of the gift to the Governor and his staff
unless the private entities direct and control the use of the payment as a gift to the
Governor and his staff.
3. So long as the donors and donations comply with rqgulati on 18944.2, the
payments will not be "gifts" to public officials.
4. As long as the donors and donations comply with regulation 189442, it will
not change the conclusion to question 3, even ifthe payments are made to the economic
development and trade promotion account established by the Secretary of Business,
Transportation and Housing.
FACTS
The California Protocol Foundation is a nonprofit organization that is exempt
from taxation under section 501(c[3) of the Intemal Revenue Code- According to its
articles of incorporatiorl the formdation "engage[sJ in chmitable and educational
activities. -.that lessen the burdens of government." The formdation was established in
2OOl and raises funds to conduct a vmiety of activities. For examplg the foundation has
conducted receptions for foreign dignrtaries, conducted trade meetings, and promoted
California to foreign countries- These functions have reduced the need of the state budget
to conduct these activities at a time of limited state resources-
In firtherance of the foundation's goals, the formdation would like to provide
financial assistance to the administration to support the administration's recently-
announced plans to conduct a trade mission to Japan in mid-November. While the
foundation would provide funding for the trade mission, the foundation would not
necessarily designate or request that any specific person in the administration go on the
trade mission- The foundation would pay for the kavel, subsistence, and lodging for some
or all of the administration officials on the trade mission. Although an exact budget has
not been set, the foundation may pay for a charterd jet, and the formdation anticipates
that the total cost could approach five hundred thousand dollars.
The foundation would fund the trip from its existing funds, supplemented by
donations that would be solicited from a variety ofprivate sources, including businesses
with an interest in trade with Asia, and individuals, other foundations, and businesses
with no specific interest in trade with Asia or in the trade mission itself. It is expected


File No. A-04-219
Page No. 3
that the donations would range from $10,000 to $50,000. The donors would understand
that their donations may be used for the trade mission, or that their donations may be
used for other foundation activities. The donors will not designate or request that any
specific person in the administration go on the trade mission. Some ofthe donors may
also go on the trade mission and they would separately pay for their share of expenses.
Because the foundation is organized under section 501(cX3) of the lnternal Revenue
Code, the contributors will expect that their donations to the foundation will be tax
deductible as a charitable expense.
In the alternativg CPF would solicit payments to the economic development and
tradepromotion account established by the Secretary of Business, Transportation and
Housing "for the purposes ofpromoting international trade and investneint.-
ANALYSIS
1. Pa5rments for Travel
The Act prohibits certain public officials ftom aaepting grfls from any single
source in any calendar year with a total value ofmore than $340-2 (Section 89503; reg.
18940.2.) The Act also states that any person who makcs a gift in violation of the gift
Iimit is liable in a civil action for an amount of up to three times the amormt of the
unlawful gift- (Section 89521.) A glft is defined as "anypayrnent that confers apersonal
benefit on the recipien! to the extent that consideration of equal or g eater value is not
received-" (Section 82028(a).) The Governor and designated ernployees in the
Governor's office are subject to the gift limits ofthe Act. (Section 89503(a) and (c).)
However, where a gift is a gift of travel, section 89506 oontrols wklrer the gift is
subject to the gift limit. (Section 89506(b).) Section 89506 prcvides:
'(a) Payments, advances, or reimbrrsem€nts, for travel, including
actual transportation and related lodging and subsisence that is reasonably
related to a legislative or governmental purpose, or to an issue of statg
national, or intemational public policy, arenot prohibited or limited by
this chapter if either of the following apply:
'(1) The havel is in connection with a speech gvex! by the erected
state officer, local elected officeholder, candidate for electod state office or
local electd office, an individual specified in section B7z0f.,mernber of a
state board or commission, or designated errployee of a state or local
government agency, the lodging and subsistence expenses are limited to
the day immdiately precedin& the day o{ and the day immediately
following the speectr, and the havel is within the Unitd States-
2 The gift limit is adjusted biennially to reflect changes in the ConsumerPrice Index.
18940.2(a).)
(Regulation


FileNo. A-04-219
Page No. 4
"(2)The travel is provided by a government, a governmental
agency, a foreign government, a governmental authority, abona fide
public orprivate educational institution, as defined in Section 203 ofthe
Revenue and Taxation code, a nonprofit organization that is exempt from
taxation under section 501(c)(3) ofthe Internal Revenue code orby a
person domiciled outside the united States which substantially satisfies
the requirements for tax exempt status under Section 501(c)(3) of the
Internal Revenue Code."
It appears that the trade mission described in your letter is reasonably related to an
issue of state, national, or international public policypursuant to the exception in section
89506. Mor@ver, you stated that the CPF is a charitable organization formed pursuant to
Internal Revenue Code Section 501(c)(3). Consequently, the gift will be exempt from the
limits of the Act.3
2. Third Party Pa5rments for Travel
In determining the treatrnent of the paSrments, it is necessary to det€rmine who the
souroe of the payments is. We have advised that under some circrmrstances it is
appropriate to pierce through an organization which appcars tobe the source of a gift, to
the actual donors of fimds usd to provide the gift. We have said that donors of gifts who
utilize intermediary organizations for channeling donations to officials are the true donors
to the officials. (Katz Advice Letter,No. I-90-269; HarperAdvice Letter, No. 4-87-088.)
"A person is the source of a gift if theperson makes a grft to an official and is not
acting as an intenrnediary-" (Regulation lS9a5(a).) If aperson makes apayrnent to a third
party and in fact directs and controls the use of the payment to make a grft to a clemly
identified official, the person is the source of the gift to the official. @egulation
189a5(a[l).) Thus, the CPF urill be considered t]re sorrce of the gift to the Governor and
his staffunless the private entities direct and control the use of the payment to make a gft
to the Governor and his staff. So long as this is not the case, the source ofthe gift to thi
Governor and his staffis the CPF, and not the private e,ntities.
3. Gifts to an Agency
Regulation 18944.2 provides that a payment which is a gift will be deemed a gft
to a public ag€,rlcy, and not a gift to a public official, if all of the following rquirements
are met:
"(I) The agency receives and controls thepaynent
"(2) Thepayment is used forofficial agencybusiness-
, 
3 The receip of a gift of free travel and accommodations may still be reportable and may result in
a conflict of interest for the officials in future decisionmaking. However, tnese oUtlgations would be on the
official, and not the donor and therefore, we cannot advise you with respect to these issues.


FileNo. A-04-219
Page No. 5
"(3) The agency, in its sole discretion, determines the specific
official or officials who shall use the payment. However, the donormay
identiff a specific purpose for the agency's use of the pa5rment, so long as
the donor does not designate the specific official or officials who may use
the payment.
"(4) The agency memorializes the payment in a written public
record which embodies the requirements of suMivisions (aXl) to (aX3) of
this regulation set forth above and which:
*(A) Identifies the donor and the official, officials, or class of
officials receiving or using the payment;
*(B) Describ€s the official agsrcy use and the nature and amormt
ofthepayment; and
'(c) Is filed l+.ith the agency official who maintains the records of
the agemc/s statements of economic interests wherethe agemcyhas a
specific office for the maintenance of such statements, or where no
specific office exists for the maintenance of such statements, at a
designatod office of the agency, and the filing is done within 30 days of
the receipt of the payment by the agency."
With regard to zuMivision (a{3), a donormaynot designate the specific official
or officials who may receive or use a pa5mrent. (Rood Adice Ietter, No. A-(D-261-) In
the past, we have advised that even if a payment was not eamrarked by invitation for use
by a specific official or officials, other facts may indicate that the papreint was so
earmarked; the totality of the surrounding circumstances are considered and not the
invitations alone. (Rood, supra; Kaye Adrrice letter, No. A-93490.) As noted above, it
appears that facts zuggest that speific officials may receive the paymemts and therefore
this exception would not apply.
If the donors and donations did complywith regulationlSg44-2, it would not
change the conclusion to question 3 even if the payments are made to the economic
development and trade promotion account established by the Secretary ofBusiness,
Transportation and Housing rather than the Goyernor's office. We have concluded that
payments from the economic development and tradepromotion acoormt fall within the
exernption of section 89506 and me not reportable by the Governor. (KolkqAdvice
Letter, No. A-98-067.)


File No. A-04-219
Page No. 6
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel
d\tO.UJJt .L
g
John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division
